title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Evening Shift
question:
Molar conductivity of a weak acid HQ of concentration 0.18 M was found to be $1 / 30$ of the molar conductivity of another weak acid HZ with concentration of 0.02 M . If $\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Q}^{-}$happened to be equal with $\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Z}^{-}$, then the difference of the $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of the two weak acids $\left(\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HZ})\right)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ (Nearest integer).

[Given: degree of dissociation $(\alpha) \ll 1$ for both weak acids, $\lambda^{\circ}$ : limiting molar conductivity of ions]
solution:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})=\mathrm{C}_1 \alpha_1^2 & \alpha_1=\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{HQ})}{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}(\mathrm{HQ})} \\ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HZ})=\mathrm{C}_2 \alpha_2^2 & \alpha_2=\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{HZ})}{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}(\mathrm{HZ})} \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HZ})}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_1}{\mathrm{C}_2} \cdot\left(\frac{\alpha_1}{\alpha_2}\right)^2=\frac{0.18}{0.02} \cdot\left[\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{HQ})}{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{HZ})}\right]^2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})}=9 \times\left(\frac{1}{30}\right)^2=\frac{1}{100} \\ & \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HZ})=2 \end{aligned} $$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Morning Shift
question:
Electricity is passed through an acidic solution of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ till all the $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ was exhausted, leading to the deposition of 300 mg of Cu metal. However, a current of 600 mA was continued to pass through the same solution for another 28 minutes by keeping the total volume of the solution fixed at 200 mL . The total volume of oxygen evolved at STP during the entire process is $\_\_\_\_$ mL . (Nearest integer)

[Given:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s}) \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{red}}^{\mathrm{o}}=+0.34 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{red}}^{\mathrm{o}}=+1.23 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

Molar mass of $\mathrm{Cu}=63.54 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

Molar mass of $\mathrm{O}_2=32 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

Faraday Constant $=96500 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$

Molar volume at $\mathrm{STP}=22.4 \mathrm{~L}$ ]
solution:
Eq of $\mathrm{Cu}=\mathrm{Eq}$ of $\mathrm{O}_2$

In electrolysis, the number of equivalents deposited and liberated are equal for the same quantity of electricity.

For copper deposition: $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}$, so $n$-factor $=2$.

For oxygen evolution (reverse of given reduction): $2\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\rightarrow \mathrm{O}_2+4\mathrm{H}^+ +4e^-$, so $n$-factor $=4$.

So, equivalents of Cu deposited $=$ equivalents of $\mathrm{O}_2$ evolved during the first part.

$$ \frac{300 \times 10^{-3} \times 2}{63.54}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2} \times 4 $$

$$ 2.36 \times 10^{-3}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2} $$

When current is further passed

After all $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ is exhausted, the current now causes only water oxidation, producing $\mathrm{O}_2$ at the anode.

Charge passed in 28 minutes at 600 mA:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2} \times 4=\frac{600 \times 28 \times 60}{96500 \times 1000} \\\\ & \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2}=2.611 \times 10^{-3} \end{aligned} $$

Total $\mathrm{O}_2$ released

Total moles of oxygen $=(2.36+2.611)\times 10^{-3}$.

Volume at STP $=n \times 22400\ \mathrm{mL}$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\left[10^{-3} \times(2.36+2.611)\right] \times 22400 \mathrm{ml} \\\\ & =111.35 \mathrm{ml} \end{aligned} $$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Evening Shift
question:
A volume of x mL of 5 M NaHCO3 solution was mixed with 10 mL of 2 M H2CO3 solution to make an electrolytic buffer. If the same buffer was used in the following electrochemical cell to record a cell potential of 235.3 mV, then the value of x = ______ mL (nearest integer).

Sn(s) | Sn(OH)62− (0.5 M) | HSnO2− (0.05 M) | OH− | Bi2O3(s) | Bi(s)

Consider up to one place of decimal for intermediate calculations


$\left[\begin{array}{ll}\text { Given: } & E_{Sn\left( {OH} \right)_6^{2 - } |HSnO_2^ -}^o = - 0.9V \\ & \mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}{ }_{\mathrm{Bi}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \mid \mathrm{Bi}}=-0.44 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \mathrm{pKa}_{\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3\right)}=6.11 \\ & \frac{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{F}}=0.059 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \text { Antilog }(1.29)=19.5\end{array}\right]$
solution:
For the half-cells (25 °C):

(1) Sn(IV)/Sn(II) couple (given $E^\circ=-0.90\text{ V}$)

Balanced reduction in basic medium:

$ \mathrm{Sn(OH)_6^{2-}+2e^- \rightarrow HSnO_2^-+3OH^-+H_2O} $

$ E_{\text{Sn}}=E^\circ_{\text{Sn}}-\frac{0.059}{2}\log\!\left(\frac{[HSnO_2^-][OH^-]^3}{[Sn(OH)_6^{2-}]}\right) $

Given $[Sn(OH)_6^{2-}]=0.5$, $[HSnO_2^-]=0.05$:

$ \frac{[HSnO_2^-]}{[Sn(OH)_6^{2-}]}=\frac{0.05}{0.5}=0.1 $

(2) Bi$_2$O$_3$/Bi couple (given $E^\circ=-0.44\text{ V}$)

Reduction:

$ \mathrm{Bi_2O_3(s)+3H_2O+6e^- \rightarrow 2Bi(s)+6OH^-} $

$ E_{\text{Bi}}=E^\circ_{\text{Bi}}-\frac{0.059}{6}\log([OH^-]^6)=E^\circ_{\text{Bi}}-0.059\log[OH^-] $

Bi has higher $E^\circ$, so it is the cathode. Thus

$ E_{\text{cell}}=E_{\text{cathode}}-E_{\text{anode}}=E_{\text{Bi}}-E_{\text{Sn}} $

Substitute and simplify:

$ E_{\text{cell}}=\left(-0.44-0.059\log[OH^-]\right)-\left(-0.90-\frac{0.059}{2}\log(0.1[OH^-]^3)\right) $

$ E_{\text{cell}}=0.46 -0.059\log[OH^-]+\frac{0.059}{2}\left(\log 0.1 + 3\log[OH^-]\right) $

Since $\log 0.1=-1$:

$ E_{\text{cell}}=0.46+\frac{0.059}{2}(-1) +\left(-0.059+\frac{3\cdot 0.059}{2}\right)\log[OH^-] $

$ E_{\text{cell}}=0.4305+0.0295\log[OH^-] $

Given $E_{\text{cell}}=0.2353\text{ V}$:

$ 0.2353=0.4305+0.0295\log[OH^-] \Rightarrow \log[OH^-]=\frac{-0.1952}{0.0295}\approx -6.6 $

So $pOH\approx 6.6 \Rightarrow pH\approx 14-6.6=7.4$.

Buffer calculation (H$_2$CO$_3$/HCO$_3^-$)

Henderson–Hasselbalch:

$ pH=pK_a+\log\frac{[HCO_3^-]}{[H_2CO_3]} $

$ 7.4=6.11+\log\frac{[HCO_3^-]}{[H_2CO_3]} \Rightarrow \log\frac{[HCO_3^-]}{[H_2CO_3]}=1.29 \Rightarrow \frac{[HCO_3^-]}{[H_2CO_3]}=19.5 $

Moles mixed:

  • $n(H_2CO_3)=2\,\text{M}\times 10\,\text{mL}=2\times 0.010=0.020\ \text{mol}$

  • $n(HCO_3^-)=5\,\text{M}\times x\,\text{mL}=5\times \frac{x}{1000}=0.005x\ \text{mol}$

Ratio $=19.5$:

$ \frac{0.005x}{0.020}=19.5 \Rightarrow 0.005x=0.39 \Rightarrow x=78 $

Answer: $\boxed{78\ \text{mL}}$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Evening Shift
question:
For strong electrolyte $\Lambda_m$ increases slowly with dilution and can be represented by the equation

$$\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - A c^{1/2}$$

Molar conductivity values of the solutions of strong electrolyte AB at 18°C are given below :

c [mol L-1]0.040.090.160.25
$\Lambda_m$ [S cm2 mol-1]96.195.795.394.9

The value of constant A based on the above data [in S cm2 mol-1/(mol/L)1/2] unit is ________.
solution:
We use the given relation for a strong electrolyte:

Using equation : $\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0-\mathrm{A} \sqrt{\mathrm{c}}$

Now substitute the first set of data: $c = 0.04$ and $\Lambda_m = 96.1$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & 96.1=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0-\mathrm{A} \sqrt{0.04} \\ & 96.1=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0-\mathrm{A} \times 0.2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & 95.7=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0-\mathrm{A} \times \sqrt{0.09} \\ & 95.7=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0-\mathrm{A} \times 0.3 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

Now subtract equation (ii) from equation (i) so that $\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0$ cancels out:

From eq. (1) and eq. (2)

$$ \begin{aligned} (96.1 - 95.7) &= (\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0 - A \times 0.2) - (\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0 - A \times 0.3) \\ 0.4 &= -0.2A + 0.3A \\ 0.4 &= 0.1A \\ A &= 4 \end{aligned} $$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Morning Shift
question:
Consider the following redox reaction taking place in acidic medium

$$ \mathrm{BH}_4^{-}(a q)+\mathrm{ClO}_3^{-}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{BO}_3^{-}(a q)+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(a q) $$

If the Nernst equation for the above balanced reaction is

$$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ln \mathrm{Q}, $$

then the value of $n$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .(Nearest integer)
solution:
Balance by ion–electron method in acidic medium.

Oxidation half-reaction (borohydride to dihydrogen borate):

Start: $\mathrm{BH_4^- \rightarrow H_2BO_3^-}$

Balance O by adding water:

$$\mathrm{BH_4^- + 3H_2O \rightarrow H_2BO_3^-}$$

Balance H by adding $\mathrm{H^+}$:

Left H = $4+6=10$, right H = $2$, so add $8\mathrm{H^+}$ on RHS:

$$\mathrm{BH_4^- + 3H_2O \rightarrow H_2BO_3^- + 8H^+}$$

Balance charge by adding electrons (oxidation ⇒ $e^-$ on RHS):

LHS charge $=-1$, RHS charge $=-1+8=+7$, so add $8e^-$ to RHS:

$$\mathrm{BH_4^- + 3H_2O \rightarrow H_2BO_3^- + 8H^+ + 8e^-}$$

So, electrons lost $=8$.

Reduction half-reaction (chlorate to chloride):

$$\mathrm{ClO_3^- \rightarrow Cl^-}$$

Balance O by adding water:

$$\mathrm{ClO_3^- \rightarrow Cl^- + 3H_2O}$$

Balance H by adding $\mathrm{H^+}$ on LHS:

$$\mathrm{6H^+ + ClO_3^- \rightarrow Cl^- + 3H_2O}$$

Balance charge by adding electrons (reduction ⇒ $e^-$ on LHS):

LHS charge $=6-1=+5$, RHS charge $=-1$, so add $6e^-$ to LHS:

$$\mathrm{6e^- + 6H^+ + ClO_3^- \rightarrow Cl^- + 3H_2O}$$

So, electrons gained $=6$.

Make electrons equal (LCM of $8$ and $6$ is $24$):

Multiply oxidation half by $3$ and reduction half by $4$:

Oxidation $\times 3$ gives $24e^-$, reduction $\times 4$ consumes $24e^-$.

After adding and canceling common species, the balanced overall reaction becomes:

$$\mathrm{3BH_4^- + 4ClO_3^- \rightarrow 3H_2BO_3^- + 4Cl^- + 3H_2O}$$

Hence, the number of electrons transferred in the balanced reaction is

$$n = 24.$$

So, $n = 24$ (nearest integer).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Evening Shift
question:
Consider the following two half-cell reactions along with the standard reduction potential given :

$\mathrm{CO}_2 + 6\mathrm{H}^+ + 6e^- \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH} + \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\qquad E_{\text{red}}^{\circ} = 0.02\ \mathrm{V}$

$\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 + 2\mathrm{H}^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\qquad E_{\text{red}}^{\circ} = 1.23\ \mathrm{V}$

A fuel cell was set up using the above two reactions such that the cell operates under the standard condition of 1 bar pressure and 298 K temperature. The fuel cell works with 80% efficiency. If the work derived from the cell using 1 mol of $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH}$ is used to compress an ideal gas isothermally against a constant pressure of 1 kPa, then the change in the volume of the gas,

$\Delta V = \underline{\hspace{2cm}}$ m$^3$. (nearest integer)

Given : $\mathrm{F} = 96500~\mathrm{C~mol}^{-1}$
solution:
For the fuel cell, methanol will be oxidised and oxygen will be reduced.

Given reduction half-cells:

$$ \mathrm{CO}_2 + 6\mathrm{H}^+ + 6e^- \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH} + \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} \qquad E^\circ_{\text{red}} = 0.02\ \mathrm{V} $$

$$ \frac{1}{2}\mathrm{O}_2 + 2\mathrm{H}^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} \qquad E^\circ_{\text{red}} = 1.23\ \mathrm{V} $$

Since methanol is the fuel, it will undergo oxidation. So we reverse the first reaction:

$$ \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH} + \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 + 6\mathrm{H}^+ + 6e^- $$

Its oxidation potential is:

$$ E^\circ_{\text{ox}} = -0.02\ \mathrm{V} $$

Now calculate standard cell potential:

$$ E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} + E^\circ_{\text{anode(ox)}} $$

$$ E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 1.23 + (-0.02) = 1.21\ \mathrm{V} $$

Number of electrons transferred:

$$ n = 6 $$

So maximum electrical work from 1 mol of $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH}$ is

$$ w_{\max} = nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}} $$

$$ w_{\max} = 6 \times 96500 \times 1.21 $$

$$ w_{\max} = 700590\ \mathrm{J} $$

Fuel cell works with $80\%$ efficiency, so actual work obtained is

$$ w = 0.8 \times 700590 $$

$$ w = 560472\ \mathrm{J} $$

This work is used to compress an ideal gas isothermally against constant pressure $P = 1\ \text{kPa} = 1000\ \text{Pa}$.

For compression against constant external pressure,

$$ w = P\Delta V $$

So,

$$ \Delta V = \frac{w}{P} $$

$$ \Delta V = \frac{560472}{1000} = 560.472\ \mathrm{m}^3 $$

Nearest integer:

$$ \Delta V \approx 560\ \mathrm{m}^3 $$

Since it is compression, volume decreases. So strictly,

$$ \Delta V = -560\ \mathrm{m}^3 $$

If only magnitude is asked, then

$$ \boxed{560} $$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Morning Shift
question:
An electrochemical cell is constructed using half cells in the direction of spontaneous change:

Fe(OH)2(s) + 2e− → Fe(s) + 2OH−(aq)      Eθ = −0.88 V

and AgBr(s) + e− → Ag(s) + Br−(aq)      Eθ = +0.07 V

Which of the following option is correct?
options: Fe is reduced in the electrochemical cell
solution:
For a spontaneous electrochemical cell, the half-reaction with higher standard reduction potential undergoes reduction, and the other one undergoes oxidation.

Given half-reactions:

$$ \mathrm{Fe(OH)_2(s) + 2e^- \rightarrow Fe(s) + 2OH^-(aq)} \qquad E^\theta = -0.88\ \text{V} $$

$$ \mathrm{AgBr(s) + e^- \rightarrow Ag(s) + Br^-(aq)} \qquad E^\theta = +0.07\ \text{V} $$

Since $+0.07\ \text{V} > -0.88\ \text{V}$, the $\mathrm{AgBr/Ag}$ half-cell will undergo reduction and the iron half-cell will undergo oxidation.

So reverse the iron half-reaction:

$$ \mathrm{Fe(s) + 2OH^-(aq) \rightarrow Fe(OH)_2(s) + 2e^-} $$

Multiply the silver half-reaction by $2$:

$$ \mathrm{2AgBr(s) + 2e^- \rightarrow 2Ag(s) + 2Br^-(aq)} $$

Now add them:

$$ \mathrm{Fe(s) + 2OH^-(aq) + 2AgBr(s) \rightarrow Fe(OH)_2(s) + 2Ag(s) + 2Br^-(aq)} $$

This matches Option A.

Now calculate $E^\theta_{\text{cell}}$:

$$ E^\theta_{\text{cell}} = E^\theta_{\text{cathode}} - E^\theta_{\text{anode}} $$

Here,

  • cathode: $\mathrm{AgBr/Ag}$, so $E^\theta_{\text{cathode}} = +0.07\ \text{V}$

  • anode: reduction potential of $\mathrm{Fe(OH)_2/Fe}$ is $-0.88\ \text{V}$

Therefore,

$$ E^\theta_{\text{cell}} = 0.07 - (-0.88) = 0.95\ \text{V} $$

So Option B is false.

Option C is false because Fe is oxidised, not reduced.

Option D is false because $E^\theta_{\text{cell}}$ is an intensive property, not an extensive property.

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option A}} $$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift
question:
An electrochemical cell, consist of the following two redox couples, $\mathrm{M}^{x+} (\mathrm{aq}) / \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{s})\left[\mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{\Theta}=+0.15 \mathrm{~V}\right]$ and $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq}) / \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})\left[\mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{\Theta}=-0.036 \mathrm{~V}\right]$. The cell EMF $\left(\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}\right)$ is recorded to be 0.2057 V . If the reaction quotient of the electrochemical reaction is found to be $10^{-2}$, then the value of $x$ is

$\_\_\_\_$ .(Nearest integer)

[Given : M is a p-block metal and $\frac{2.303 R T}{F}=0.059 \mathrm{~V}$ ]
solution:
For the cell, the two reduction potentials are:

$$ \mathrm{M}^{x+} + x e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{M}(s), \qquad E^\circ_{\text{red}} = +0.15\,\text{V} $$

and

$$ \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + 3 e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}(s), \qquad E^\circ_{\text{red}} = -0.036\,\text{V} $$

Since the reduction potential of $\mathrm{M}^{x+}/\mathrm{M}$ is greater, it will act as the cathode.

So,

  • Cathode: $\mathrm{M}^{x+} + x e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{M}(s)$

  • Anode: $\mathrm{Fe}(s) \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + 3e^-$

Hence,

$$ E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}} = 0.15 - (-0.036) = 0.186\,\text{V} $$

Now use the Nernst equation:

$$ E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.059}{n}\log Q $$

Given:

$$ E_{\text{cell}} = 0.2057\,\text{V}, \qquad Q = 10^{-2} $$

Since $\log 10^{-2} = -2$,

$$ 0.2057 = 0.186 - \frac{0.059}{n}(-2) $$

$$ 0.2057 = 0.186 + \frac{0.118}{n} $$

$$ 0.2057 - 0.186 = \frac{0.118}{n} $$

$$ 0.0197 = \frac{0.118}{n} $$

$$ n = \frac{0.118}{0.0197} \approx 5.99 \approx 6 $$

So the number of electrons transferred in the balanced cell reaction is

$$ n = 6 $$

Now, for balancing electrons:

  • Oxidation of iron gives $3$ electrons

  • Reduction of $\mathrm{M}^{x+}$ consumes $x$ electrons

Therefore, total electrons transferred in the balanced equation will be $\mathrm{LCM}(3,x)$.

Given this is $6$, we get:

$$ \mathrm{LCM}(3,x)=6 $$

Possible values of $x$ are $2$ or $6$.

Now it is given that $\mathrm{M}$ is a p-block metal. Common p-block metals generally form ions like $2^+$, $3^+$, $4^+$, etc. Among the possible values, the most suitable is

$$ x=2 $$

So, the nearest integer value of $x$ is

$$ \boxed{2} $$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift
question:
One half cell in a voltaic cell is constructed by dipping silver rod in $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$ solution of unknown concentration, other half cell is Zn rod dipped in 1 molar solution of $\mathrm{ZnSO}_4$.

A voltage of 1.60 V is measured at 298 K for this cell. What is the concentration of $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ions used in terms of $\log x\left(x=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\right)$?

$$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\ominus}=-0.76 \mathrm{~V}, \quad \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\ominus}=+0.80 \mathrm{~V}, \frac{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{~F}}=0.059 \mathrm{~V} $$
solution:
For the cell,

  • anode: $\mathrm{Zn} \rightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$

  • cathode: $\mathrm{Ag}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{Ag}$

So the overall cell reaction is

$$ \mathrm{Zn} + 2\mathrm{Ag}^+ \rightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+} + 2\mathrm{Ag} $$

Now, standard cell potential:

$$ E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}} $$

$$ E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 0.80 - (-0.76) = 1.56 \text{ V} $$

Given:

$$ E_{\text{cell}} = 1.60 \text{ V} $$

Using Nernst equation at $298\ \text{K}$:

$$ E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.059}{n}\log Q $$

Here, $n=2$.

Reaction quotient:

$$ Q=\frac{[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\mathrm{Ag}^+]^2} $$

Since $\mathrm{ZnSO}_4$ is $1$ M,

$$ [\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}] = 1 $$

Let

$$ [\mathrm{Ag}^+] = x $$

Then

$$ Q=\frac{1}{x^2} $$

Substitute in Nernst equation:

$$ 1.60 = 1.56 - \frac{0.059}{2}\log\left(\frac{1}{x^2}\right) $$

Now,

$$ \log\left(\frac{1}{x^2}\right) = -2\log x $$

So,

$$ 1.60 = 1.56 - \frac{0.059}{2}(-2\log x) $$

$$ 1.60 = 1.56 + 0.059\log x $$

$$ 1.60 - 1.56 = 0.059\log x $$

$$ 0.04 = 0.059\log x $$

$$ \log x = \frac{0.04}{0.059} = \frac{4}{5.9} $$

Hence, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{\frac{4}{5.9}} $$

So, Option B is correct.

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift
question:
At 298 K , the molar conductivity of $x \%(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w}) \mathrm{MX}$ solution (aqueous) is $123.5 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The conductance of same solution is $1.9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~S}$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-2}$.

(Given: cell constant $=1.3 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$; molar mass of MX is $75 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, density of aqueous solution of MX at 298 K is $1.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$ )
solution:
Conductivity ($\kappa$) is related to the conductance ($G$) and the cell constant ($G^*$) by the following relationship:

$$ \kappa = G \times G^* $$

Substituting the given values:

$$ \kappa = (1.9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~S}) \times (1.3 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}) $$

$$ \kappa = 2.47 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} $$

The molar conductivity ($\Lambda_m$) is related to the conductivity ($\kappa$) and the molarity ($C$ in $\mathrm{mol~L}^{-1}$) by the formula:

$$ \Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa \times 1000}{C} $$

Rearranging the formula to solve for molarity ($C$):

$$ C = \frac{\kappa \times 1000}{\Lambda_m} $$

Substituting the values into the equation:

$$ C = \frac{(2.47 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}) \times 1000 \mathrm{~cm}^3 \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}{123.5 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}} $$

$$ C = \frac{2.47}{123.5} \mathrm{~mol \mathrm{~L}^{-1}} $$

$$ C = 0.02 \mathrm{~mol \mathrm{~L}^{-1}} $$

An $x \% (\mathrm{w}/\mathrm{w})$ aqueous solution means there are $x \mathrm{~g}$ of the solute (MX) in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of the solution.

Using the density of the solution, we can find the volume of $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of the solution:

$$ \text{Volume of solution} = \frac{\text{Mass of solution}}{\text{Density}} $$

$$ \text{Volume} = \frac{100 \mathrm{~g}}{1.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}} = 100 \mathrm{~mL} $$

Molarity is defined as the number of moles of solute per $1000 \mathrm{~mL}$ (1 liter) of the solution.

Since $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of the solution contains $x \mathrm{~g}$ of MX, $1000 \mathrm{~mL}$ of the solution will contain $10x \mathrm{~g}$ of MX.

Now, calculate the number of moles of MX in $1000 \mathrm{~mL}$, which is exactly our molarity ($C$):

$$ \text{Moles in 1 L} = \frac{\text{Mass of solute in 1 L}}{\text{Molar mass of solute}} $$

$$ C = \frac{10x}{75} $$

We already calculated the molarity to be $0.02 \mathrm{~mol \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}$. Equating the two values:

$$ \frac{10x}{75} = 0.02 $$

$$ 10x = 0.02 \times 75 $$

$$ 10x = 1.5 $$

$$ x = 0.15 $$

The problem requires $x$ to be expressed in the form of $\_\_\_\_ \times 10^{-2}$.

$$ x = 0.15 = 15 \times 10^{-2} $$

The value of $x$ is 15.

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift
question:
For a general redox reaction

Anode $\quad \operatorname{Red}_1 \rightarrow \mathrm{Ox}_1^{\mathrm{n}_1^{+}}+\mathrm{n}_1 \mathrm{e}^{-}$

Cathode $\mathrm{O} x_2+\mathrm{n}_2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \operatorname{Red}_2^{\mathrm{n}_2-}$

Which of the following statement is incorrect?
options: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { The overall reaction can be written as } \mathrm{n}_2 \operatorname{Red}_1+\mathrm{n}_1 \mathrm{O} x_2 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{n}_2 \mathrm{O} x_1^{\mathrm{n}_1^{+}}+\mathrm{n}_1 \operatorname{Red}_2^{\mathrm{n}_2^{-}} \\ & \text {} \end{aligned} $$
The electrons do not appear in the overall reaction because electrons produced at the anode are consumed at the cathode.
Here $n$ is the number of electrons transferred in redox reaction.
If the reaction is carried out reversibly, the electrical work done is equal to the ratio of charge and potential difference through which charge is moved.
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { }\\ &\begin{aligned}(a)\,\, Anode:\,\, & \left(R{e d 1} \longrightarrow O_{x_1}^{n^{+}}+u_1 e^{-}\right) \cdot n_2 \\ & \left(Ox_{2}+n_2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \operatorname{Red}_2^{n_2^{-}}\right) \cdot n_1 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

To add the anode and cathode half-reactions, the number of electrons must be the same on both sides.

So, we multiply the anode reaction by $n_2$ and the cathode reaction by $n_1$, so that both have $n_1 n_2$ electrons.

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & n_2 \operatorname{Red}_1 \longrightarrow n_2 \mathrm{Ox}_1^{n+}+n_2 n_1 e^{-} \\ & n_1 \mathrm{Ox}_2+n_1 n_2 e^{-} \longrightarrow n_1 \operatorname{Red}_2^{n_2-} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Overall ren: } n_2 R{e d}+n_1 O x_2 \longrightarrow n_2 O x^{n+}+n_1 R{e d_ 2}^{n_2-} \end{aligned} $$

Now, when we add these two equations, the $n_1 n_2 e^{-}$ on the product side of the anode reaction cancels with the $n_1 n_2 e^{-}$ on the reactant side of the cathode reaction.

(b) The $e^{-}$produced at the anode are consumed at the cathode, that's why $e^{-}$do not appear in the overall reaction.

This statement is correct because electrons cancel during addition of the two half-reactions.

(c) We know,

$$ \begin{aligned} & E = E^{o}-\frac{R T}{n f} \log Q \\ & E-E^{\circ}=\frac{-R T}{n f} \log Q \\ & \frac{E-E^{\circ}}{R T / f}=-\frac{1}{n} \log Q \end{aligned} $$

Here, $E$ changes with $\log Q$ according to the Nernst equation, and $n$ is the number of electrons transferred in the balanced redox reaction.

plotting a $y=m x+c$ graph,

$$ \begin{aligned} & y=\frac{E-E^0}{R T / F} \\ & x=\log Q \\ & \text { slope }(m)=-\frac{1}{n} \\ & \text { slope } \propto \frac{1}{n} \end{aligned} $$

So, the slope is $-\frac{1}{n}$. This statement is also correct.

$$ \text { Where, } n=\text { no. of } e^{-} \text {transfered in redox reaction. } $$

(D) The electrical work done by a galvanic cell is given by the product of the total charge transfered and the potential difference, not their ratio.

This statement is correct because electrical work is given by $W = qV$ (charge multiplied by potential difference).

$$ \begin{aligned} W_{electrical} & =\text { charge × Potential difference. } \\ & =Q \times V \end{aligned} $$

                $$ W_e=I t \times V $$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Morning Shift
question:
$$ \text { Consider the following data. } $$

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline \text { Electrolyte } & \wedge^{\circ}_\mathbf{m}{\mathbf{(}} \mathbf{S ~ c m}^{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{~ m o l}^{\mathbf{1}} \mathbf{)} \\ \hline \mathrm{BaCl}_2 & x_1 \\ \hline \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 & x_2 \\ \hline \mathrm{HCl} & x_3 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$\mathrm{BaSO}_4$ is sparingly soluble in water. If the conductivity of the saturated $\mathrm{BaSO}_4$ solution is $x \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ then the solubility product of $\mathrm{BaSO}_4$ can be given as (Here $\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}=\wedge^{\circ}_{\mathrm{m}}$ )
solution:
$ \mathrm{BaSO_4 (s)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ba^{2+} (aq)} + \mathrm{SO_4^{2-} (aq)} $

If the solubility of $\mathrm{BaSO_4}$ is $s \, \text{mol L}^{-1}$, then in solution:

$ [\mathrm{Ba^{2+}}] = s, \quad [\mathrm{SO_4^{2-}}] = s $

Thus, the solubility product is

$ K_{\text{sp}} = [\mathrm{Ba^{2+}}][\mathrm{SO_4^{2-}}] = s^2 $

Relation between molar conductivity and observed conductivity

By definition,

$ \kappa = \frac{\Lambda_m \, c}{1000} $

where

  • $\kappa$ = conductivity in $\text{S cm}^{-1}$,

  • $\Lambda_m$ = molar conductivity in $\text{S cm}^2 \text{mol}^{-1}$,

  • $c$ = molar concentration in $\text{mol L}^{-1}$.

For the saturated solution of $\mathrm{BaSO_4}$,

$ \kappa = x $

and

$ c = s $

Therefore,

$ \Lambda_m^{\text{(BaSO}_4)} = \frac{1000x}{s} $

Determining $\Lambda_m^{\circ}$ for $\mathrm{BaSO_4}$

We can find $\Lambda_m^{\circ}$ for $\mathrm{BaSO_4}$ using Kohlrausch’s law of independent migration of ions.

According to the law,

$ \Lambda_m^{\circ}(\text{Electrolyte}) = \text{sum of molar ionic conductivities of its ions}. $

Now, from the data:

$ \begin{aligned} \Lambda_m^{\circ}(\mathrm{BaCl_2}) &= \lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{Ba^{2+}}} + 2\lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{Cl^-}} = x_1 \\ \Lambda_m^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}) &= 2\lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{H^+}} + \lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{SO_4^{2-}}} = x_2 \\ \Lambda_m^{\circ}(\mathrm{HCl}) &= \lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{H^+}} + \lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{Cl^-}} = x_3 \end{aligned} $

To get $\Lambda_m^{\circ}(\mathrm{BaSO_4}) = \lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{Ba^{2+}}} + \lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{SO_4^{2-}}}$,

we combine the above equations:

$ (x_1 + x_2 - 2x_3) = (\lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{Ba^{2+}}} + 2\lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{Cl^-}}) + (2\lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{H^+}} + \lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{SO_4^{2-}}}) - 2(\lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{H^+}} + \lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{Cl^-}}) $

Simplifying,

$ x_1 + x_2 - 2x_3 = \lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{Ba^{2+}}} + \lambda^{\circ}_{\mathrm{SO_4^{2-}}} = \Lambda_m^{\circ}(\mathrm{BaSO_4}) $

Thus,

$ \Lambda_m^{\circ}(\mathrm{BaSO_4}) = x_1 + x_2 - 2x_3 $

Equating conductivities

Since it is given that $(\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^{\circ})$ for the saturated solution:

$ \Lambda_m^{\circ} = \frac{1000x}{s} $

$ s = \frac{1000x}{\Lambda_m^{\circ}} = \frac{1000x}{x_1 + x_2 - 2x_3} $

Expression for solubility product

$ K_{\text{sp}} = s^2 = \left(\frac{1000x}{x_1 + x_2 - 2x_3}\right)^2 = \frac{10^6 x^2}{(x_1 + x_2 - 2x_3)^2} $

Final Answer

$ \boxed{K_{\text{sp}} = \dfrac{10^6 x^2}{(x_1 + x_2 - 2x_3)^2}} $

Hence, Option A is correct.

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift
question:
Given at 298 K :

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}}^{\ominus}=\mathrm{X} \text { Volt } \\ & \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}}^{\ominus}=\mathrm{Y} \text { Volt } \end{aligned} $$

The $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}^{\ominus}$ in Volt at 298 K is given by :
solution:
(i) $\mathrm{Fe}^{+2}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s}) \ldots \ldots . . \Delta \mathrm{G}_1^{\circ}$

(ii) $\mathrm{Fe}^{+3}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s}) \ldots \ldots . . \Delta \mathrm{G}_2{ }^{\circ}$

(iii) $\mathrm{Fe}^{+3}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{+2}(\mathrm{aq}) \ldots \ldots . . \Delta \mathrm{G}_3^{\circ}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { iii }=\text { ii }-\mathrm{i} \\ & -1 \times \mathrm{F}\times \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}^{\circ}=-3 \times \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{y}-(-2 \times \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{x}) \\ & \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}^{\circ}=3 \mathrm{y}-2 \mathrm{x} \end{aligned} $$

Option (B) is correct
